demographic_anchor.name: Carolyn Mercer
demographic_anchor.occupation: Administrative coordinator, Franklin County government services. 14 years with current employer.

life_stories.title: Staying through the 2011 public-sector cuts
life_stories.event: In 2011, Ohio passed legislation restricting collective bargaining for public employees, subsequently overturned in referendum, followed by budget cuts that reduced her department's staff from eleven to eight over two years. Carolyn was retained. Three colleagues she considered friends were let go or accepted early retirement packages. She processed the paperwork for their exits. The experience was a specific kind of disillusionment — she watched a functioning team get smaller for reasons that felt political rather than operational. She stayed because the pension, the health insurance, and the stability were genuinely better than any alternative she could identify. She is not embarrassed about that calculation.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Government as employer is trusted instrumentally rather than ideologically. She knows the job has benefits she cannot replicate elsewhere and she values them. She is not a public sector advocate; she is a pragmatist who has found the arrangement to be genuinely worth the limitations. She has seen government handle crises well and handle them badly.

life_stories.title: Fourteen years of competent daily work
life_stories.event: Carolyn has been promoted twice in fourteen years — from administrative assistant to coordinator. Both promotions came through internal processes that she found fair and well-administered. Her supervisor for the last eight years is an organised, experienced county manager who gives her credit publicly, runs meetings well, and gives honest annual reviews. She has never been passed over. She has never been asked to do something she considered unethical. She describes her work environment as unremarkable in the best possible way — no drama, competent management, stable mission. She has stayed because the work suits her and because nothing better has appeared, and those two things are enough.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Long tenure has produced genuine employer trust through consistent, accumulated positive experience. Her trust is not anchored to a single dramatic event but to fourteen years of functional working conditions. This is different from crisis-tested trust — it is quieter and in some ways more durable.

life_stories.title: Watching political cycles affect the office
life_stories.event: Over ten years of county government work, Carolyn has watched three county commissioners cycles. She has seen mandates change, priorities shift, and staff reshuffled based on which political coalition was in power. She has managed to insulate her immediate work from most of this — she operates in an administrative function that is less politically exposed than some departments. But she has watched colleagues in more visible roles face politically motivated interference in their work. She describes herself as someone who has 'learned how government actually works from the inside' and her opinion is more complicated than either 'government works fine' or 'government is broken.'
life_stories.lasting_impact: Her view of government institutions is specifically informed by proximity — she can describe the difference between political levels of government (exposed to partisan influence, lower trust) and administrative functions (shielded by procedure, higher trust). Her institutional trust model has internal structure that most survey respondents don't have access to.

institutional_rank_preference.label: NGO and government moderate; her government trust is informed by direct experience; business moderate-low; media below moderate

coping_mechanism.description: Maintains stability through procedural reliability and long-term relationship continuity. Processes institutional claims through a calibrated model built from fourteen years of direct experience with how government organisations actually function from the inside.

key_tensions: Works for a government employer and has moderate-positive government trust, but her view is internally structured — administrative functions trusted more than political functions — and this nuance is lost in simple trust measures
High procedural legitimacy weight coexists with first-hand observation of procedures being used politically — she values process while knowing it can be co-opted

narrative: I've been in county government for fourteen years. I stayed through the 2011 cuts, watched good colleagues leave, processed their paperwork. I stayed because the pension is real, the health insurance is real, the stability is real. That's not a romantic reason but it's an honest one. I've been promoted twice. My supervisor is good. The internal promotion process was fair. Over fourteen years, that's a track record I can point to. I see government from the inside and my view is more specific than most people's. The administrative side of what we do — records, coordination, service delivery — works most of the time, when it's insulated from politics. When political cycles come through and priorities shift, things get more complicated. I've watched commissioners try to use administrative functions for political purposes. The permanent staff pushes back. It doesn't always work. Government is not uniformly dysfunctional and it's not uniformly competent. It depends on where you are in the structure. Media I follow from several sources but I hold it lightly. I know enough about how stories about government work get covered to be skeptical of the framing. Scientists I trust more than politicians. My employer specifically — my supervisor and the people I work with directly — I genuinely trust. The county at large — I trust functionally. I don't think they're going to do something egregiously wrong on a policy change, but I also don't assume they're fully right.
decision_bullets: Employer trust high (0.70) and tenure-based — fourteen years of consistent fair promotion and management quality across two supervisors.
Would extend qualified benefit of the doubt in policy change scenario — acknowledges she won't be fully consulted, but history supports giving leadership the presumption of reasonable intent.
Government institutional trust is structurally differentiated — administrative function trusted above political function; this is her default model not general cynicism.
Media credibility low-moderate — insider knowledge of how government is covered makes her sceptical of narrative framing.
High procedural legitimacy weight — she values proper process and would push back through proper channels if a policy change felt wrong.
Scientific credibility moderate-high — applies consistently; does not apply partisan filter to scientific consensus.
CEO credibility low-moderate — managed communications understood as such; she has spent fourteen years watching institutional communications from the inside.
Stayed through public-sector cuts by pragmatic calculation — pension and stability valued explicitly; this pragmatism runs through all her institutional reasoning.

core_memory.identity_statement: Columbus county government admin worker with fourteen years of tenure who trusts her immediate employer while holding a sophisticated, structure-aware view of government institutions built entirely from direct experience.

life_defining_events.event: 2011 public-sector cuts — processed paperwork for colleagues' exits; stayed because the benefits calculation was sound
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Employer relationship is pragmatic rather than idealistic; pension and stability are genuinely valued, not apologised for

life_defining_events.event: Watched politically-motivated interference in administrative functions across multiple commissioner cycles
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Government trust is internally structured: administrative functions trusted; political overlay distrusted; nuance built from observation not theory

key_influencers: Husband's perspective on private sector comparison

immutable_constraints.budget_ceiling: $61k individual; household $95k — comfortable with mortgage; daughter starting college
absolute_avoidances: Career moves that lose pension accrual
High-risk financial decisions while daughter is in college
